coredata.dc:title: Correlated color temperature alteration with changing the position of carbon dot film for warm WLEDs
coredata.dc:creator: Park, Sung Jun
Yang, Hyun Kyoung
Moon, Byung Kee
coredata.dc:description: Commercial white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) composed on the blue InGaN chip and YAG:Ce3+ phosphor have been used as a promising illumination sources. WLEDs with an adjustable correlated color temperature (CCT) are very important to the wide variety of applications. Herein, we represent a facile synthetic strategy to prepare green emission carbon dots by solvothermal method using citric acid and urea as a precursor. The carbon dots have a size about 4 nm and emission peak centered at 532 nm. The fabricated WLEDs present white light with variable color temperature from warm (5602 K) to cool white (9242 K) by combining YAG:Ce3+ and carbon dot films. Thus, the results show that the fabricated carbon dot and carbon dot film can be used in white sources in the next generation of lighting application as a CCT converter.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
White LED
CCT converter
Flexible film

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea(NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT; Ministry of Science and ICT) ( 2020R1A2C400219311 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM, (b) FE-TEM image, (c) FT-IR spectrum, (d) absorption spectrum, (e) PL spectra of the prepared carbon dots, and (f) photographs of the prepared carbon dots solutions under day light, UV light (365 nm), blue light (460 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) XPS spectrum of carbon dots, High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) N 1s of the carbon dots.

(a) photographs of carbon dot film under sunlight and blue light (460 nm), (b) Cross-sectional SEM image of the fabricated carbon dot film, (c) absorbance spectrum, (d) transmittance spectruma of carbon dot film. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) EL spectra of blue LD (405 nm), blue LD/glass, blue LD/PVA film, and blue LD/carbon dot film, (b1-4) images of blue LD, blue LD/glass, blue LD/PVA film, and blue LD/carbon dot film, (c) EL spectra of blue LED chip (460 nm), blue LED chip/glass, blue LED chip/PVA film, and blue LED chip/carbon dot film, (d1-4) images of blue LED chip, blue LED chip/glass, blue LED chip/PVA film, and blue LED chip/carbon dot film. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Photograph and optical microscope surface image of YAG:Ce3+ film, (b) optical microscope image of the cross-section of YAG:Ce3+ film, (c) XRD pattern, (d) PLE spectrum, and PL spectrum of YAG:Ce3+ film.

(a) EL spectra of blue LED chip, (b) blue LED chip/YAG:Ce3+ film, (c) blue LED chip/YAG:Ce3+ film/PVA film, (d) blue LED chip/YAG:Ce3+ film/carbon dot film, (e) blue LED chip/PVA film/YAG:Ce3+ film, and (f) blue LED chip/carbon dot film/YAG:Ce3+ film. . (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
CIE coordination diagram of the three fabricated LED devices.

head.ce:title: Correlated color temperature alteration with changing the position of carbon dot film for warm WLEDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of LED Convergence Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48547, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of LED convergence Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48547, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department Electrical, Electronics, and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department Electrical, Electronics, and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of LED Convergence Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48547, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Commercial white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) composed on the blue InGaN chip and YAG:Ce3+ phosphor have been used as a promising illumination sources. WLEDs with an adjustable correlated color temperature (CCT) are very important to the wide variety of applications. Herein, we represent a facile synthetic strategy to prepare green emission carbon dots by solvothermal method using citric acid and urea as a precursor. The carbon dots have a size about 4 nm and emission peak centered at 532 nm. The fabricated WLEDs present white light with variable color temperature from warm (5602 K) to cool white (9242 K) by combining YAG:Ce3+ and carbon dot films. Thus, the results show that the fabricated carbon dot and carbon dot film can be used in white sources in the next generation of lighting application as a CCT converter.

ce:section.ce:para: The WLEDs consist of a blue InGaN chip and YAG:Ce3+ phosphor. White light emitting diodes (WLEDs) are known as a new generation light source for our daily life and have been applied in displays, and lighting because of their fast response, long lifetime, environment protection, and highly efficient conversion properties[1–5]. However, the WLEDs have several disadvantages such as high correlated color temperature (CCT) due to strong blue intensity. Blue light from 200 to 400 nm has become a big problem because it badly affects human health such as eye diseases (photokeratitis, pinguecula, cataract, climatic droplet keratopathy, and macular degeneration, etc.) and skin diseases (photoaging, sunburn, skin cancer, and erythema) and induces eye fatigue, decrease of color reproduction and color clarity[6–9]. To resolve these problems, there are several methods which either employ an increase of the red region or a decrease of the blue.

Fluorescent materials include organic dyes, inorganic quantum dots, perovskite phosphors, and inorganic phosphors which have several flaws[10–14]. Such materials may possess complicated synthesis processes and include toxic starting materials such as cadmium, lead, sulfide, selenide, nitrate, sulfate, fluoride, etc. [15–20] However, the carbon dots, as a luminescent nanoparticle comprising carbonaceous structure with several functional groups are free of heavy metal and have good photostability, simple synthesis method, strong luminescence, tunable excitation and emission, and low toxicity. Therefore, researchers have studied the carbon dots which can be applied to various applications in many fields such as light-emitting diodes (LEDs), sensing, bioimaging, solar cell, and optical films [21–26].

In this work, we synthesize carbon dots and carbon dot film by using a facile solvothermal method without any complex and harsh conditions. The prepared carbon dots and carbon dot film exhibit the strong green emission and ultraviolet and blue absorbance. The structure, optical properties of the prepared carbon dots and carbon dot films were analyzed through FE-TEM, FT-IR, Raman, XPS, UV–visible, PL spectrum. WLEDs were fabricated which consist of a commercial YAG film and prepared carbon dot film exhibiting different CCTs (5602–9242 K) and colors (cool to warm white emission).

Citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5%) and urea (CO(NH2)2, 98%) were purchased from DUKSAN Co. and DAEJUNG Chemicals, respectively. Dimethylformamide (DMF, C3H7NO,99.5%) was purchased from DUKSAN Co. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, (C2H4O)n) powders with an average molecule weight 1500 were purchased from Junsei Co. InGaN chip was emission peak about 450 nm, operating under voltage of 3.0 V.

Citric acid (2 g, 9.52 mmol), urea (4 g, 61.54 mmol), and DMF (20 mL) were put into a beaker and stirred with a magnetic stirrer for 0.5 h. The prepared solution was sealed into a stainless-steel autoclave with a Teflon liner, heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the obtained carbon dot solution was filtered to remove impurities with using a 0.2 μm membrane filter. The solution was refined using a 3000 Da dialysis bag (MWCO 3000 Da, Spectra/Por6) for 24 h. Finally, a yellow aqueous dispersion containing carbon dots were obtained.

Poly(vinyl alcohol) powder (PVA, MW = 1500 g/mol) (1 g) was dissolved in deionized water (DI-water) (20 mL) under vigorous stirring using a magnetic stirrer at 90 °C until a transparent solution and then cooled down to room temperature. Subsequently, the prepared carbon dot solution was added into the PVA solution and the mixtures were stirred for 1 h to obtain homogeneous solution. The mixtures (5 mL) dropped into a petri dish using the pipet and dry at 90 °C for 1 day. Flexible carbon dot film was obtained.

Field emission transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM) of the carbon dots was performed on a JEM-2100. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of carbon dots was measured on a Perkin-Elmer (JASCO, FT-4100) spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the prepared carbon dots were measured on a Kratos Axis Ultra X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos Analytical Ltd.) using an Al Kα radiation source. The Raman spectrum of the prepared carbon dots was conducted on a JASCO NRS-5100 spectrometer with a 532 nm laser. The digital photographs of carbon dot film were obtained by using the Cannon DSLR EOS 100D digital camera with Sigma 50 mm F2.8 EX DG Macro lens under sunlight and UV light (365 nm). The thickness and surface of carbon dot film were examined by field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JEOL, JSM-6700). UV–Visible spectra of the prepared carbon dot and carbon dot film have been recorded on a (JASCO, V-670). The luminescence property of the prepared carbon dots was conducted at room temperature by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PTI). The thickness and surface of YAG:Ce3+ film were observed by using optical microscopy. The structure of YAG:Ce3+ film was investigated using the X-ray diffraction (XRD) using PANalytical X’PERT PRO powder X-ray diffractometer with a CuKα beam at 40 kV and 30 mA. The chip spectra of the commercial YAG:Ce3+ film, carbon dot film, and PVA film were measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer (SM245, Korea Spectral Products). The CCT values of the fabricated WLEDs were obtained from the CIE 1931 coordinate diagram by using color calculator program of OSRAM.

Fig. 1 (a) depicts the TEM image of the prepared carbon dots, which reveals a uniform shape with an average diameter of about 4 nm. The FE-TEM image of the prepared carbon dots shows lattice fringes at 0.21 nm, corresponding to the graphene (100) planes which is sp2 hybrid carbon in carbon dots as depicted in Fig. 1(b) [27]. To confirm chemical structure and functional groups, FT-IR spectroscopy of the prepared carbon dots was employed. The FT-IR spectrum shows several absorption bands with the peaks at 3211 cm−1 (stretching vibration O–H), 2921 cm−1 (stretching vibration C–H), 1649 cm−1 (stretching vibration C–O), 1557 cm−1 (bending vibration N–H), 1390 cm−1 (stretching vibration CC), 1070 cm−1 (stretching vibration C–O), and 678 cm−1 (C–H) are represented in Fig. 1(c) [28].

Fig. 1(d) represents the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the carbon dots. The absorption spectrum shows the two typical absorption peaks in the region of 200–500 nm which is π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domain (CC) and n-π* transitions of CO bond, respectively.

The excitation-dependent PL spectra of the prepared carbon dots showed the strongest peak at around 532 nm under an excitation wavelength of 420 nm as depicted in Fig. 1(e). The emission peaks of carbon dots slightly shift emission center to long wavelength, indicating that the carbon dots. When the excitation wavelength was increased from 340 to 500 nm, the PL intensity of carbon dots increased to a maximum and then decreased with increasing the excitation wavelength from 340 to 500 nm. The maximum emission peak position is dependent of the excitation wavelength. The stokes shift of the prepared carbon dot is about 25–172 nm. The quantum yield of the prepared carbon dot is 22.7% (excitation wavelength 340 nm), 28.1% (excitation wavelength 360 nm), 24.0% (excitation wavelength 380 nm), 23.8% (excitation wavelength 400 nm), 15.6% (excitation wavelength 420 nm), 14.2% (excitation wavelength 440 nm), 10.0% (excitation wavelength 460 nm), 9.8% (excitation wavelength 480 nm). The solution color of prepared carbon dots is green under sunlight irradiation and green under UV and blue light irradiation as depicted in Fig. 1(f).

To examine the chemical bonding and elemental composition of carbon dot, the XPS spectra were obtained. The elements analysis of XPS spectra appears that the carbon dots consist of three predominant peaks at 285.2 eV (C1s) and at 532.7 eV (O1s), and 400.7 eV (N1s) as shown in Fig. 2 . Their relative contents were 52.57% (C), 6.01% (O), and 41.42% (N) on the basis of calculations of the integral area. High-resolution spectra of C 1s were fitted into three distinct carbon state of (C–C/CC), (C–O/C–N), and (CO) at 285.0 eV, 286.7 eV, and 288.5 eV, respectively as depicted in Fig. 2(b) [29]. The O 1s spectra show two peaks at 532.5 and 534.0 eV, corresponding to CO and C–O, respectively as depicted in Fig. 2(c). The spectrum of N 1s show three peaks at 399.9, 400.4, and 400.9 eV, representing pyridinic, pyrrolic, and quaternary, respectively as depicted in Fig. 2(d). Fig. S1 displays the Raman spectrum of the prepared carbon dots which have two bands. The D band at 1358 cm−1 for the edge defects of carbon and G band at 1603 cm−1 for the sp2 carbon, respectively. The relatively high intensity of G band compared to the D band of carbon dots indicates the high crystallinity of the carbon dots.

Fig. 3 (a) depicts the photographic images of carbon dot film under sunlight and blue light. The color of the fabricated carbon dot film appears yellow green under sunlight and has a green emission under blue light. To confirm the microstructure of the fabricated carbon dot film, the cross-section SEM image of the fabricated carbon dot film was as shown in Fig. 3(b). The thickness of carbon dot film is about 35 μm. The UV–visible spectrum of the prepared carbon dot film was depicted in Fig. 3(c). The absorption spectrum of carbon dot film represents the several absorption peaks in the region of 200–500 nm which is assigned to the PVA absorbance peak, π-π* and n-π* transitions of carbon dots, respectively. Fig. 3(d) shows the transmittance of the carbon dot film between 200 and 450 nm in the range of 20–40%. The inset in Fig. 3(d) depicts the images of the PVA film and carbon dot film which represent the highly transparent.

To verify the ability of the fabricated carbon dot film as a blocking film, the spectrum of blue laser diode (405 nm), blue laser diode/glass, blue laser diode/PVA film, and blue laser diode/carbon dot film was represented in Fig. 4 (a). The blue intensity of blue laser diode under glass or PVA film is similar to the intensity of blue laser diode. It means that the glass and PVA film alone do not affect the intensity of the blue region. In the case of the blue laser diode/carbon dot film, the intensity of blue light was significantly decreased and green fluorescence centered at 525 nm appear. The reason is that the carbon dot film has a strong blue absorption and blue excitation based on the above results (UV–visible spectrum and PL spectrum). Fig. 4(b1-4) represents the images of the glass, PVA film, and carbon dot film on the blue laser diode (405 nm). In case of the glass, blue light is almost penetrated. Therefore, the transmitted blue light intensity is similar with the intensity of blue laser diode. When covering the blue laser diode with using PVA film, the transmitted blue light intensity is weaker than glass. In the case of the fabricated carbon dot film, the transmitted blue light intensity is significantly decreased by absorbance and excitation region of carbon dots. Fig. 4(c) shows the spectra of blue LED chip (460 nm), blue LED chip/glass, blue LED chip/PVA film, and blue LED chip/carbon dot film. The blue light intensity of blue LED chip mounted on glass or PVA film compared to blue LED chip slightly decreases. In the case of the blue LED chip/carbon dot film, the blue light intensity is significantly reduced. The blue blocking ratio of glass and PVA film is less than 10% and carbon dot film is over 60% as depicted in Fig. 4(c) inset. The blue blocking ratio of the carbon dot film is the higher than glass and PVA film. Fig. 4(d1-4) shows the images of glass, PVA film, and carbon dot film on the blue LED chip (460 nm). In case of the fabricated carbon dot film, we can observe blocking of blue light.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the photograph of the YAG:Ce3+ film surface through optical microscopy. Tens of micrometer sized phosphor particles were embedded in YAG:Ce3+ film. The thickness of the YAG:Ce3+ film is about 470 μm as shown in Fig. 5(b). Fig. 5(c) represents the XRD patterns of the YAG:Ce3+ film. There are no impurity diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern of the YAG:Ce3+ film compared with YAG:Ce3+ JCPDS Card. The PLE and PL spectrum of the YAG:Ce3+ film are shown in Fig. 5(d). The PLE spectrum of the YAG:Ce3+ film has two excitations centered at 400 and 450 nm originated from the 4f→5d2 and 4f→5d1 transition of YAG:Ce3+. The PL spectrum the YAG:Ce3+ film shows the yellow emission band centered at 545 nm under 450 nm excitation. The emission band represents a broad nonsymmetrical band for Ce3+ which is the transitions of the 5d excited state to the two ground state configurations of 2F7/2 and 2F5/2 of Ce3+, respectively [30].

To verify the applicability of the fabricated carbon dot films, a carbon dot film was prepared and the optical property of blue LED chip, blue LED chip/YAG film, blue LED chip/YAG film/PVA film, blue LED chip/YAG film/carbon dot film was measured with changing the position of the films. Fig. 6 shows the chip spectra of the (a) blue LED chip, (b) blue LED chip/YAG film, (c) blue LED chip/YAG film/PVA film, (d) blue LED chip/YAG film/carbon dot film, (e) blue LED chip/PVA film/YAG film, and (f) blue LED chip/carbon dot film/YAG film. The main peak of the blue LED chip is centered at 460 nm. The spectrum of blue LED chip/YAG film shows the spectrum of general white LED which combine blue and yellow color. In that case of blue LED chip/YAG film/PVA film (Fig. 6(c)) and blue LED chip/PVA film/YAG film (Fig. 6(e)), the spectra are almost identical because the PVA film does not affect the spectrum of YAG:Ce3+ film. The spectrum of the blue LED chip/YAG film/carbon dot film shows that the intensity of yellow region is higher than blue region as shown in Fig. 6(d). The reason may be that the intensity of blue region is relatively lower than yellow region because the blue region already absorbed in YAG:Ce3+ is absorbed by the carbon dots once more. In that case of blue LED chip/carbon dot film/YAG film (Fig. 6(f)), the spectrum of the blue LED chip/carbon dot film/YAG film shows that the intensity of yellow region is higher than blue region. Fig. 7 represents the CIE color coordinates of the three fabricated WLEDs. The CCT of the blue LED chip/YAG film, blue LED chip/carbon dot film/YAG film, and blue LED chip/YAG film/carbon dot film is 9242, 8156, and 5602 K. To sum up, the above results reveal that CCT of the fabricated LED (blue LED chip/YAG film/carbon dot film) is lower than that of LED (blue LED chip/carbon dot film/YAG film). The foregoing results indicate that the fabricated LED (blue LED chip/YAG film/carbon dot film) can be realized as a warm white LED.

In conclusion, we have synthesized the green emitting carbon dots and carbon dot film by a facile method. The synthesized carbon dots and carbon dot film have strong green emission under UV excitation of 420 nm. The absorption of carbon dots and carbon dot film was represented from UV to blue region. The blue blocking ratio of the carbon dot film is over 60%. It means that the light of blue region was absorbed well. The fabricated WLEDs devices with using the fabricated carbon dot film exhibited a tunable CCT from 9242 K (cool white) to 5602 K (warm white). According to the above results, the prepared carbon dots and carbon dot film can be applied for several applications such as CCT converter and absorption film.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea(NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT; Ministry of Science and ICT) (2020R1A2C400219311).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2020.109063.

Sung Jun Park: received his M.S. in 2017 from Pukyong National University. He is currently pursuing for his Ph.D. under supervision of Profs. Hyun Kyoung Yang in School of LED convergence Engineering, Pukyong National University. His current research interests are in the area of functional inorganic and organic materials (e.g., phosphors, biomaterials, photocatalyst materials, and carbon dots).

Hyun Kyoung Yang: is a professor at LED convergence Engineering, Pukyong National University. He received his Ph.D. degree in Physics at Pukyong National University. In 2012, he joined the Korea Institute of Energy Research (KIER). In 2014, he now is guiding research group of around 6 people at Pukyong University aiming at developing functional materials and ceramics (e.g., composite materials, luminescent materials, phosphor in glass, and heat sink).

Byung Kee Moon: is a professor in department of Physics at Pukyong National University. He received his Ph.D. degree in Physics at Pusan National University. His research interests are in the area of nanostructured inorganic and organic materials and their optical properties including their synthesis, structural and microscopic analysis.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Precise tuning of the thickness and optical properties of highly stable 2D organometal halide perovskite nanosheets through a solvothermal process and their applications as a white LED and a fast photodetector

Giri, Precise Tuning of the Thickness and Optical Properties of Highly Stable 2D Organometal Halide Perovskite Nanosheets through a Solvothermal Process and Their Applications as a White LED and a Fast Photodetector, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 12 (2020) 6283-6297
